replication_study.hypothesis: Among university students, higher motivation for distance learning is associated with lower odds of being in the "severe" category of psychological distress (K10 ≥ 30). Operationally: in a logistic regression of severe distress (1 = severe, 0 = not-severe) on categorical levels of motivation (Likert 1–4), the odds-ratio for the highest vs. lowest motivation category will be < 1.

data_plan.source_type: Online self-administered questionnaire (Qualtrics link circulated on social media)
data_plan.wave_or_subset: Full de-identified dataset of 766 responses (includes cases beyond the 381 used in the published paper)
data_plan.access_details: Provided directly in replication_data folder as CSV; no further restrictions.

qualification.explanation: The file contains the same measurement instrument (10-item Kessler scale and motivation item) used in the original study, allowing an exact methodological replication while drawing on an enlarged, independently coded sample.
qualification.similarity_to_original: 1) Same K10 items (variables Kessler_1–Kessler_10) and motivation item (online_learning_1) as described in the original methods section (post_registration.json → method → description). 2) Same sampling frame—Jordanian university students recruited via Facebook/WhatsApp—as noted in post_registration.json → data → source.
qualification.deviation_from_original: 1) The original analysis retained only 381 complete cases (post_registration.json → data → sample_size), whereas the replication dataset contains 766 entries (dataset inspection via get_dataset_info). 2) The replication file is de-identified and appears to include additional respondents and potentially repeated attempts, therefore forming a new empirical sample.

data_plan.notes: Variable names are generic and require computation of K10 total and distress categories. Missingness is present for individual K10 items (~5-15 %). No explicit gender or age columns are included in this stripped version, so the replication focuses on the focal motivation predictor only.

planned_method.steps: 1) Load CSV from /app/data. 2) Compute K10 total score. 3) Classify distress into None/Mild/Moderate/Severe with standard cut-offs (<20, 20-24, 25-29, ≥30). 4) Recode motivation Likert (1–4) as categorical. 5) Drop rows with missing K10 items or motivation. 6) Fit logistic regression: severe ~ C(motivation). 7) Extract odds-ratios, SEs, CIs, p-values. 8) Save table to /app/data/replication_results.csv.
planned_method.models: Binary logistic regression (statsmodels Logit); optional robustness check with multinomial logit for all distress categories.
planned_method.control_variables: None in primary model (age/gender absent in dataset).

planned_estimation_and_test.estimation: Odds-ratio for each motivation category relative to the highest motivation reference
planned_estimation_and_test.test: Wald z-test on logistic coefficients (two-sided, α = 0.05)

planned_method.missing_data_handling: List-wise deletion on any missing K10 item or motivation response
planned_method.multiple_testing_policy: Not required – single focal predictor; no adjustment
planned_method.inference_criteria: Replication considered successful if the coefficient for low motivation is negative (OR < 1) and p < 0.05, matching the directionality of the original OR = 0.45.

files.replication_data/replicate_analysis.py: End-to-end script that loads the CSV from /app/data, computes K10 totals & distress categories, runs logistic regression with statsmodels, and exports a results table to /app/data/replication_results.csv.

codebase.notes: The script is fully self-contained and uses only open-source Python libraries. All paths are hard-coded to the container’s data mount (/app/data) per instructions.

analysis.comparison_metrics: Compare the estimated odds-ratio for lowest vs highest motivation to the original OR = 0.45 in magnitude (< 1) and statistical significance (p < 0.05). Secondary: sign and confidence-interval overlap.
